sections.title: Join Types
sections.description: All join strategies available in PySpark. Understanding each type's behavior, row selection logic, and physical execution is critical for correct and performant pipelines.

entries.title: Inner Join
entries.subtitle: Returns only matching rows from both sides
entries.description: The most common join type. Returns rows where the join key exists in BOTH DataFrames. Rows from either side that have no match in the other are excluded entirely. In SQL: the classic JOIN or INNER JOIN.
entries.when_to_use: Use when you only care about records that have a corresponding match. Example: orders that have a matching customer, transactions with a matching product. If a record has no match, you don't want it.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice', 100),
    (2, 'Bob',   200),
    (3, 'Carol', 300),
], ['order_id', 'customer', 'amount'])

customers = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice', 'NY'),
    (2, 'Bob',   'CA'),
    # Carol has no customer record!
], ['order_id', 'name', 'state'])

result = orders.join(customers, on='order_id', how='inner')
result.show()
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left DF
        L1[order 1: Alice]
        L2[order 2: Bob]
        L3[order 3: Carol]
    end
    subgraph Right DF
        R1[customer 1: Alice]
        R2[customer 2: Bob]
    end
    L1 -->|match| R1
    L2 -->|match| R2
    L3 -.->|no match - DROPPED| X[x]
    style L3 fill:#7f1d1d
    style X fill:#7f1d1d
edge_cases: NULL keys never match — rows with NULL on the join key are excluded from the result even if both sides have NULL.
Duplicate keys on the right side create a Cartesian product for those keys.
Inner join on multiple columns: df1.join(df2, on=['key1', 'key2'], how='inner')

entries.title: Left Outer Join
entries.subtitle: All rows from left, matched rows from right
entries.description: Returns ALL rows from the left DataFrame, plus the matched row from the right when a match exists. When there's no match on the right, the right side columns are filled with NULL. Also called LEFT JOIN in SQL.
entries.when_to_use: Use when the left DataFrame is your 'source of truth' and you want to enrich it with optional data from the right. Example: get all customers and their latest order (if they have one). Customers without orders still appear.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
users = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (3, 'Carol'),  # No orders
], ['user_id', 'name'])

orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Laptop', 999),
    (2, 'Phone',  499),
], ['user_id', 'product', 'price'])

result = users.join(orders, on='user_id', how='left')  # or how='left_outer'
result.show()
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left DF - ALL rows kept
        L1[user 1: Alice]
        L2[user 2: Bob]
        L3[user 3: Carol]
    end
    subgraph Right DF
        R1[order: Laptop]
        R2[order: Phone]
    end
    L1 -->|match| R1
    L2 -->|match| R2
    L3 -->|no match - NULL fill| N[right cols = NULL]
    style N fill:#1e3a5f
edge_cases: To find records with NO match on right side: filter where a right-side column is NULL after the join.
df1.join(df2, 'key', 'left').filter(df2['col'].isNull()) — finds unmatched left rows.
If right side has duplicate keys, left rows that match will be duplicated.

entries.title: Right Outer Join
entries.subtitle: All rows from right, matched rows from left
entries.description: Mirror of Left Outer Join. Returns ALL rows from the right DataFrame, with NULL for unmatched left columns. Less common than LEFT — usually you can swap DataFrames and use LEFT instead, which is more intuitive.
entries.when_to_use: Use when the right DataFrame is the authoritative source. In practice, swapping DataFrames and using left join is usually cleaner and easier to reason about.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Laptop', 999),
    (2, 'Phone',  499),
], ['user_id', 'product', 'price'])

users = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (3, 'Carol'),  # No orders
], ['user_id', 'name'])

# Right join: ALL users appear regardless of orders
result = orders.join(users, on='user_id', how='right')
result.show()
edge_cases: Equivalent to: users.join(orders, 'user_id', 'left') — right join is rarely necessary.
NULL padding happens on the LEFT side columns for unmatched right rows.

entries.title: Full Outer Join
entries.subtitle: All rows from both sides, NULLs where no match
entries.description: Returns ALL rows from BOTH DataFrames. Rows that have a match are combined. Rows with no match on the other side have NULL filled for the missing columns. Also called FULL JOIN or FULL OUTER JOIN in SQL.
entries.when_to_use: Use for data reconciliation: comparing two sources to find what's in one but not the other, or what's in both. Common in ETL pipelines for change detection (SCD Type 2).
entries.codigo_pyspark:
source_a = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (3, 'Carol'),   # only in A
], ['id', 'name_a'])

source_b = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (4, 'Dave'),    # only in B
], ['id', 'name_b'])

result = source_a.join(source_b, on='id', how='outer')  # or 'full' or 'fullouter'
result.show()
entries.salida_consola:
+---+------+------+
| id|name_a|name_b|
+---+------+------+
|  1| Alice| Alice|  <- matched
|  2|   Bob|   Bob|  <- matched
|  3| Carol|  null|  <- only in A
|  4|  null|  Dave|  <- only in B
+---+------+------+
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left DF
        L1[id=1 Alice]
        L2[id=2 Bob]
        L3[id=3 Carol]
    end
    subgraph Right DF
        R1[id=1 Alice]
        R2[id=2 Bob]
        R4[id=4 Dave]
    end
    L1 <-->|match| R1
    L2 <-->|match| R2
    L3 -.->|no match - null right| NR[null]
    R4 -.->|no match - null left| NL[null]
    style NR fill:#1e3a5f
    style NL fill:#1e3a5f
edge_cases: Full outer join is expensive — it cannot use Broadcast Join strategy. Always results in a full shuffle.
To find rows ONLY in A: result.filter(result['name_b'].isNull())
To find rows ONLY in B: result.filter(result['name_a'].isNull())

entries.title: Cross Join (Cartesian Product)
entries.subtitle: Every row in left paired with every row in right
entries.description: Returns the Cartesian product: each row of the left DataFrame paired with EVERY row of the right DataFrame. If left has M rows and right has N rows, the result has M×N rows. No join key is needed. Extremely expensive — use with caution.
entries.when_to_use: Use to generate all combinations of two small DataFrames. Common for: generating all date-product combinations for a reporting skeleton, or comparing each record against every other. ONLY use on small datasets.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
dates = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('2024-01-01',),
    ('2024-01-02',),
    ('2024-01-03',),
], ['date'])

products = spark.createDataFrame([
    ('Laptop',),
    ('Phone',),
], ['product'])

# 3 dates × 2 products = 6 rows
result = dates.crossJoin(products)
result.show()

# In SQL-style:
# spark.conf.set('spark.sql.crossJoin.enabled', 'true')  # needed for implicit cross
result2 = dates.join(products, how='cross')
edge_cases: If left=1M rows and right=1M rows → result = 1 TRILLION rows. Always check sizes first.
Spark requires spark.sql.crossJoin.enabled=true for implicit cross joins as a safety guard.
Performance: uses BroadcastNestedLoopJoin or CartesianProduct physical operator — no hash table.

entries.title: Left Semi Join
entries.subtitle: Returns left rows that have a match in right — right columns excluded
entries.description: Returns rows from the LEFT DataFrame where a match exists in the right, BUT does not include any columns from the right side. It's like an INNER JOIN but you only get the left columns. Useful as a filter based on another dataset.
entries.when_to_use: Use as an existence filter: 'Give me all customers who placed at least one order' — without duplicating customer rows for customers with multiple orders. More efficient than inner join when you don't need right-side columns.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
customers = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (3, 'Carol'),  # no orders
], ['id', 'name'])

orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Laptop'),
    (1, 'Mouse'),  # Alice has 2 orders
    (2, 'Phone'),
], ['customer_id', 'product'])

# Who placed an order? No duplicates, no right columns
result = customers.join(orders, customers['id'] == orders['customer_id'], how='leftsemi')
result.show()
entries.salida_consola:
+---+-----+
| id| name|
+---+-----+
|  1|Alice|  <-- has orders (kept, not duplicated)
|  2|  Bob|  <-- has orders
+---+-----+
# Carol excluded. Alice NOT duplicated despite 2 orders.
# Right columns (product) NOT included.
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left DF - source
        L1[id=1 Alice]
        L2[id=2 Bob]
        L3[id=3 Carol]
    end
    subgraph Right DF - existence check only
        R1[customer_id=1]
        R2[customer_id=1]
        R3[customer_id=2]
    end
    L1 -->|exists in right - KEEP| OUT1[Alice]
    L2 -->|exists in right - KEEP| OUT2[Bob]
    L3 -.->|not in right - DROP| X[x]
    style X fill:#7f1d1d
    style L3 fill:#7f1d1d
edge_cases: Result schema only contains LEFT DataFrame columns — no right columns appear.
Multiple matches on right side do NOT duplicate left rows (unlike inner join).
Equivalent SQL: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders)

entries.title: Left Anti Join
entries.subtitle: Returns left rows that have NO match in right
entries.description: The opposite of Left Semi Join. Returns rows from the LEFT DataFrame where NO match exists in the right side. Perfect for finding missing or orphaned records. Columns come from left DataFrame only.
entries.when_to_use: Use to find records that DON'T exist in another table. Example: customers who never placed an order, products with no sales, users who haven't logged in this month. Also useful for validating data completeness.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
customers = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice'),
    (2, 'Bob'),
    (3, 'Carol'),  # no orders
    (4, 'Dave'),   # no orders
], ['id', 'name'])

orders = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Laptop'),
    (2, 'Phone'),
], ['customer_id', 'product'])

# Who has NEVER placed an order?
result = customers.join(orders, customers['id'] == orders['customer_id'], how='leftanti')
result.show()
entries.salida_consola:
+---+-----+
| id| name|
+---+-----+
|  3|Carol|  <-- NO orders — included
|  4| Dave|  <-- NO orders — included
+---+-----+
# Alice and Bob excluded because they DO have orders.
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Left DF
        L1[id=1 Alice]
        L2[id=2 Bob]
        L3[id=3 Carol]
        L4[id=4 Dave]
    end
    subgraph Right DF - exclusion set
        R1[customer_id=1]
        R2[customer_id=2]
    end
    L1 -.->|match found - DROP| X1[x]
    L2 -.->|match found - DROP| X2[x]
    L3 -->|no match - KEEP| OUT1[Carol]
    L4 -->|no match - KEEP| OUT2[Dave]
    style X1 fill:#7f1d1d
    style X2 fill:#7f1d1d
    style L1 fill:#7f1d1d
    style L2 fill:#7f1d1d
edge_cases: Rows with NULL join keys: NULL != NULL so NULL keys on the left will always appear in anti join results.
Equivalent SQL: SELECT * FROM customers WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders)
More efficient alternative to: df1.exceptAll(df1.join(df2, ..., 'inner'))

entries.title: Self Join
entries.subtitle: Joining a DataFrame with itself
entries.description: A self join is when you join a DataFrame with a copy of itself. Used for hierarchical relationships or comparing rows within the same dataset. Requires aliasing to distinguish the two sides.
entries.when_to_use: Use for: finding manager-employee relationships in the same table, comparing each row to other rows (e.g., finding duplicate transactions), or traversing tree/graph structures stored in one table.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

employees = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, 'Alice',  None),   # CEO, no manager
    (2, 'Bob',    1),      # reports to Alice
    (3, 'Carol',  1),      # reports to Alice
    (4, 'Dave',   2),      # reports to Bob
], ['id', 'name', 'manager_id'])

# Find each employee and their manager's name
result = employees.alias('emp') \
    .join(
        employees.alias('mgr'),
        col('emp.manager_id') == col('mgr.id'),
        how='left'
    ) \
    .select(
        col('emp.name').alias('employee'),
        col('mgr.name').alias('manager')
    )
result.show()
entries.salida_consola:
+--------+-------+
|employee|manager|
+--------+-------+
|   Alice|   null|  <-- CEO, no manager
|     Bob|  Alice|
|   Carol|  Alice|
|    Dave|    Bob|
+--------+-------+
edge_cases: Always alias both sides of a self join — without aliases Spark cannot resolve column ambiguity.
For deep hierarchies (more than 2 levels), use GraphX or iterative joins with .checkpoint() to avoid lineage explosion.
Self join with inner creates N² combinations — filter carefully.

sections.title: Window Functions
sections.description: Advanced analytical operations that calculate values across a set of rows related to the current row. Includes ranking, offsets, and rolling aggregates.

entries.title: Ranking: row_number vs rank vs dense_rank
entries.subtitle: Managing duplicates and numbering protocols
entries.description: These functions assign a sequential number to each row within a window partition. The main difference is how they handle ties (rows with the same values in order by clause).
entries.when_to_use: Use row_number for unique identifiers, rank for competition-style ties (skipping numbers), and dense_rank for consecutive ties (no gaps).
entries.syntax:
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, rank, dense_rank

w = Window.partitionBy("dept").orderBy("salary")
df.withColumn("rn", row_number().over(w))
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import row_number, rank, dense_rank

data = [(1, "A", 100), (2, "A", 100), (3, "A", 150)]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ["id", "dept", "salary"])

w = Window.partitionBy("dept").orderBy("salary")

df.select(
    "*",
    row_number().over(w).alias("row_num"),
    rank().over(w).alias("rank"),
    dense_rank().over(w).alias("dense_rank")
).show()
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Partition A
        R1[100] --> RN1[rn:1, rank:1, dense:1]
        R2[100] --> RN2[rn:2, rank:1, dense:1]
        R3[150] --> RN3[rn:3, rank:3, dense:2]
    end
edge_cases: If no orderBy is specified, row_number might be non-deterministic.
Partitioning by high-cardinality columns can cause memory pressure.

entries.title: Offset: lag vs lead
entries.subtitle: Accessing previous or next records
entries.description: Lag and Lead allow you to access data from other rows (before or after) without doing self-joins. Lag looks back N rows, Lead looks forward N rows.
entries.when_to_use: Perfect for time-series analysis: calculating month-over-month growth, detecting status changes, or finding the time difference between events.
entries.syntax:
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, lead
lag("col", offset=1, default=None).over(window)
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag, col

data = [("Jan", 100), ("Feb", 120), ("Mar", 110)]
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, ["month", "sales"])

w = Window.orderBy("month") # Simple order

df.withColumn("prev_sales", lag("sales", 1).over(w)) \n  .withColumn("diff", col("sales") - col("prev_sales")).show()
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    R1[Row 1] -->|lead| R2[Row 2]
    R2 -->|lag| R1
    R2 -->|lead| R3[Row 3]
    R3 -->|lag| R2
edge_cases: Default values are important to avoid NULLs in the first/last row.
Ordering must be strict for stable lag/lead results.

sections.title: Aggregations
sections.description: How Spark computes summary statistics across groups. Understanding Hash vs Sort Aggregate and how grouping impacts shuffle is key for large-scale data.

entries.title: Hash Aggregate
entries.subtitle: The default and fastest aggregation method
entries.description: Spark builds a hash map in memory where keys are the grouping columns and values are the running aggregates. It operates in two phases: partial aggregation (on executors) and final aggregation (after shuffle).
entries.when_to_use: The default choice for most groupBys. It is extremely fast as long as the unique groups for a partition fit in the executor memory heap.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, avg

df = spark.createDataFrame([
    ("IT", 1000), ("IT", 1500), ("Sales", 2000)
], ["dept", "salary"])

# Spark usually picks HashAggregate here
df.groupBy("dept").agg(sum("salary").alias("total")).show()
entries.salida_consola:
+-----+-----+
| dept|total|
+-----+-----+
|   IT| 2500|
|Sales| 2000|
+-----+-----+

== Physical Plan ==
*(2) HashAggregate(keys=[dept], functions=[sum(salary)])
+- Exchange hashpartitioning(dept, 200)
   +- *(1) HashAggregate(keys=[dept], functions=[partial_sum(salary)])
edge_cases: Memory pressure: If there are too many unique keys, Spark might spill to disk or fallback to SortAggregate.
High cardinality groups: Can lead to skewed shuffles.

entries.title: Sort Aggregate
entries.subtitle: The spill-safe fallback for massive groups
entries.description: Requires the data to be sorted by grouping keys. Spark then iterates through the sorted data, accumulating values for a key until it sees a new key.
entries.when_to_use: Used when grouping keys are too large for a hash map or when data is already sorted. It is slower than HashAggregate but more memory-robust as it does not require a random-access hash map.
entries.syntax:
-- Forced by Spark automatically when needed
== Physical Plan ==
SortAggregate(keys=[...])
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Not directly invokable, but Spark uses it if memory is tight
# or if the data type doesn't support hashing.
df.groupBy("very_large_column").count().explain()
entries.salida_consola:
== Physical Plan ==
SortAggregate(keys=[large_col#1], functions=[count(1)])
+- Sort [large_col#1 ASC NULLS FIRST], false, 0
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(large_col#1, 200)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    I[Unsorted Data] --> S[Sort by Key]
    S --> A[Iterate & Accumulate]
    A --> O[Output: Key + Count]
edge_cases: Additional overhead of sorting if not already sorted.
Spill-safe: Will not crash on OOM as easily as HashAggregate.

sections.title: Storage & Pushdown
sections.description: How data layout on disk affects Spark performance. Optimized formats (Parquet/Delta) allow Spark to read only the data it needs.

entries.title: Columnar vs Row Formats
entries.subtitle: Parquet/ORC/Delta vs CSV/JSON
entries.description: Columnar formats store data by column rather than by row. This is highly efficient for analytical queries (OLAP) because Spark can skip entire columns that are not in the SELECT clause.
entries.when_to_use: Always use Parquet or Delta for large-scale analytical workloads. Avoid CSV/JSON for anything other than raw landing zones.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Reading only specific columns from Parquet
df = spark.read.parquet("huge_dataset.parquet") \n    .select("id", "status") # Only these 2 columns are actually read from disk!
entries.salida_consola:
== Physical Plan ==
*(1) FileScan parquet [id#1,status#5] Batched: true, DataFilters: [], Format: Parquet, Location: ..., PartitionFilters: [], ReadSchema: struct<id:bigint,status:string>
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Parquet File
        C1[Column: Date]
        C2[Column: ID]
        C3[Column: Payload]
    end
    S[Spark Query: SELECT id] --> C2
    style C3 fill:#7f1d1d
edge_cases: Schema evolution is better handled by Delta Lake.
Row-based formats (Avro) are better for high-throughput writes/streaming.

entries.title: Predicate & Projection Pushdown
entries.subtitle: Filtering data at the storage layer
entries.description: Spark pushes the FILTER (WHERE clause) and SELECT down to the storage layer. The reader (Parquet/Delta) only returns rows and columns that match the filter, vastly reducing I/O.
entries.when_to_use: Essential for performance. Spark does this automatically if the format supports it. Ensure you partition your data by columns you frequently filter on.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Pushdown example
df = spark.read.parquet("data/") \n    .filter("amount > 1000") \n    .select("transaction_id")

df.explain()
entries.salida_consola:
== Physical Plan ==
*(1) FileScan parquet [transaction_id#1] PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(amount), GreaterThan(amount,1000)], ReadSchema: struct<transaction_id:string,amount:double>
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    D[Disk Storage] -->|Filter: amount > 1000| R[Storage Reader]
    R -->|Only 10% of data| E[Spark Executor]
    E --> Output
edge_cases: Pushdown does not work for UDFs or complex expressions.
Filter must be on a top-level column usually.

sections.title: Persistence & Caching
sections.description: How to reuse intermediate DataFrames without recomputing the entire lineage. Critical for iterative algorithms and multiple actions.

entries.title: cache() vs persist()
entries.subtitle: Managing memory and lineage
entries.description: cache() is a shorthand for persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK). It tells Spark to store the DataFrame in memory (and disk if it doesn't fit) after it is computed for the first time.
entries.when_to_use: Use when the same DataFrame is used in multiple downstream actions. Example: computing a summary and then saving the raw data.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark import StorageLevel

df = spark.read.parquet("huge_data/").filter("amount > 100")
df.cache() # Mark for caching

# First action: computes and CACHES
print(df.count())

# Second action: reads from CACHE (FAST!)
df.write.save("output/")
entries.salida_consola:
== Batch 1 (uncached) ==
Job 1: 45s

== Batch 2 (cached) ==
Job 2: 2s (Reads from Memory)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    L[Lineage] --> C[Cache Layer]
    C --> A1[Action 1]
    C --> A2[Action 2]
    A1 -->|Reads from Cache| C
    A2 -->|Reads from Cache| C

entries.title: Checkpointing
entries.subtitle: Breaking the lineage for safety
entries.description: Unlike cache, checkpoint() saves the data to a reliable storage (HDFS/S3) and TRUNCATES the lineage. This prevents lineage chains from becoming too long and causing StackOverflowErrors.
entries.when_to_use: Use in iterative algorithms (Machine Learning) or when the lineage is extremely complex. It is slower than cache but more reliable.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir("tmp/checkpoints")

df = spark.read.json("raw_data/")
# ... 50 transformations ...
df_final = df.checkpoint()

# df_final has NO lineage back to raw_data
df_final.explain()
entries.salida_consola:
== Physical Plan ==
*(1) Scan ExistingRDD [id#1,name#2]
(Lineage truncated!)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    L1[Complex Lineage] --X|Truncated| CP[Checkpoint File on Disk]
    CP --> A[Action]

sections.title: Core Optimization & AQE
sections.description: How Spark adapts its execution plan at runtime based on actual data statistics. Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is the biggest performance boost in Modern Spark.

entries.title: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)
entries.subtitle: The brain of Modern Spark (3.x+)
entries.description: AQE re-optimizes the query plan BETWEEN query stages. It uses runtime statistics from completed stages to improve the plan for remaining stages.
entries.when_to_use: Enabled by default in Spark 3.2+. Always keep it on. It handles three main problems: Coalescing partitions, Switching join strategies, and Optimizing skew joins.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# No special code needed, just check the explain plan
df1.join(df2, "id").groupBy("type").count().explain()
entries.salida_consola:
== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan(isFinalPlan=true)
+- HashAggregate(keys=[type#2], functions=[count(1)])
   +- AQEShuffleRead(coalesced)
      +- Exchange hashpartitioning(type#2, 200)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    S1[Stage 1 Done] --> R[Runtime Stats]
    R --> P[Optimizer Re-plans]
    P --> S2[Stage 2 Executed with New Plan]

entries.title: Skew Join Optimization
entries.subtitle: Handling uneven data distributions
entries.description: Data skew occurs when one partition has way more data than others, making one task take 10x longer. AQE detects this and splits the skewed partition into smaller sub-partitions.
entries.when_to_use: When you see "straggler tasks" in the Spark UI. AQE handles this automatically by looking for partitions much larger than the average.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Before AQE: 1 task takes 10 mins, others 10 secs
# After AQE: The 10-min task is split into 10 smaller tasks.
entries.salida_consola:
== physical plan ==
SortMergeJoin(skew=true)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Before AQE
        T1[Small]
        T2[HUGE SKEW]
        T3[Small]
    end
    subgraph After AQE
        T1[Small]
        T2a[Split 1]
        T2b[Split 2]
        T2c[Split 3]
        T3[Small]
    end

sections.title: Infrastructure & Deployment
sections.description: How Spark connects to cluster managers and manages application lifecycle. This is the foundation of every Spark job.

entries.title: Cluster Managers (YARN vs K8s)
entries.subtitle: Resource orchestration strategies
entries.description: Spark can run on various cluster managers. YARN is the traditional Hadoop manager, while Kubernetes (K8s) is the modern cloud-native approach.
entries.when_to_use: Use YARN for legacy on-prem Hadoop clusters. Use K8s for cloud-native, containerized environments requiring better isolation and autoscaling.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Setting master in code (not recommended for prod)
spark = SparkSession.builder 
    .master("yarn") 
    .appName("MyJob") 
    .getOrCreate()
entries.salida_consola: 24/03/27 10:00:15 INFO YarnClientImpl: Submitted application application_16123456789_0001
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    D[Driver] -->|Request Resources| CM[Cluster Manager: YARN/K8s]
    CM -->|Allocate Containers| E1[Executor 1]
    CM -->|Allocate Containers| E2[Executor 2]
    E1 <--> D
    E2 <--> D

entries.title: Client vs Cluster Mode
entries.subtitle: Where does the Driver live?
entries.description: In Client Mode, the driver runs on the machine you submit from. In Cluster Mode, the driver runs inside the cluster (on a worker node).
entries.when_to_use: Use Client Mode for interactive debugging/notebooks. Use Cluster Mode for production jobs to avoid network latency and driver failure if your local machine disconnects.
entries.codigo_pyspark: # No code change needed, only spark-submit flags
entries.salida_consola: INFO: Driver successfully started on executor node: 10.0.1.5
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Local Machine
        S[spark-submit]
    end
    subgraph Cluster
        D[Driver Node]
        W1[Worker 1]
        W2[Worker 2]
    end
    S -->|Submit| D

sections.title: Unified Memory Management
sections.description: How Spark splits executor RAM between calculation (Execution) and cache (Storage). Spark 3.x uses a unified approach.

entries.title: Execution vs Storage Memory
entries.subtitle: The dynamic boundary
entries.description: Execution Memory is for shuffles/joins. Storage Memory is for caching. They share the same pool; if one is free, the other can borrow it.
entries.when_to_use: Adjust spark.memory.fraction (default 0.6) and spark.memory.storageFraction (default 0.5) if you have heavy caching or massive shuffles.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Check settings
print(spark.conf.get("spark.memory.fraction"))
entries.salida_consola: MemoryManager: Allocated 2GB for Execution, 1.5GB for Storage (Borrowed)
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Executor JVM RAM
        subgraph Spark Memory (60%)
            E[Execution Memory]
            S[Storage Memory]
            E <-->|Eviction/Borrow| S
        end
        U[User Memory: 40%]
    end

sections.title: Structured Streaming
sections.description: Advanced concepts for processing streams of data reliably, handling state, and dealing with late-arriving records out of order.

entries.title: Watermarks & Late Data
entries.subtitle: Limiting memory usage in stateful streams
entries.description: A watermark is a trailing threshold. If the max event time seen is T, and the watermark is W, any data arriving with an event time older than (T - W) is dropped. This allows Spark to clear old state from memory.
entries.when_to_use: Mandatory anytime you use aggregations or joins over event time in Structured Streaming. Without it, state grows infinitely leading to OOM.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import window

# Allow data up to 10 minutes late
windowed_counts = df \n  .withWatermark("event_time", "10 minutes") \n  .groupBy(window("event_time", "1 minute"), "type") \n  .count()
entries.salida_consola:
WARN WatermarkTracker: Updating watermark to 2026-03-27T10:15:00
WARN FlatMapGroupsWithState: Dropped 5 rows arriving later than watermark.
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    E[Event Time:
 10:20] -->|Max Time Seen| S{Spark Engine}
    S -->|Calculates Watermark
10:20 - 10 mins = 10:10| W[Watermark: 10:10]
    L[Late Event:
 10:05] -->|Dropped! < 10:10| X((X))
    G[Good Event:
 10:15] -->|Accepted > 10:10| State[(State Store)]

entries.title: Streaming Output Modes
entries.subtitle: How Spark writes stream results
entries.description:
1. Append: Only writes NEW rows that will never change (requires Watermark for aggregations).
2. Update: Writes only the rows that were updated in the last trigger.
3. Complete: Writes the ENTIRE result table every trigger.
entries.when_to_use: Append for saving to logs. Update for writing to a database. Complete for tiny reference tables or total counts.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Writing an aggregation to Delta format
query = agg_df.writeStream 
  .outputMode("update") 
  .format("delta") 
  .option("checkpointLocation", "/tmp/checkpoints/") 
  .start("/tmp/delta_output/")
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Trigger 1: A=1, B=1
      App1[Append: A=1, B=1]
      Upd1[Update: A=1, B=1]
      Com1[Complete: A=1, B=1]
    end
    subgraph Trigger 2: A=2, C=1
      App2[Append: -WAITING WATERMARK-]
      Upd2[Update: A=2, C=1]
      Com2[Complete: A=2, B=1, C=1]
    end

sections.title: Data Governance & Security
sections.description: How Spark integrates with Catalogs to provide data discovery, schema enforcement, and fine-grained access control.

entries.title: Hive Metastore vs Unity Catalog
entries.subtitle: Centralizing tables and metadata
entries.description: A Catalog or Metastore acts as the central directory of all your datasets. It maps table names (e.g. "sales.customers") to physical files (e.g. "s3://bucket/path/parquet/"). Hive Metastore (HMS) is the legacy open-source standard. Unity Catalog is the modern enterprise standard for Lakehouses.
entries.when_to_use: Always use a Catalog in production. Never rely purely on file paths in code. It decouples the physical storage from the logical table, allowing seamless migrations.
entries.syntax: spark.sql("CREATE TABLE db.table USING delta LOCATION 's3://...'")
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Querying a cataloged table using Spark SQL API
df = spark.table("prod.sales.european_orders")

# Listing metadata dynamically via Catalog API
spark.catalog.listDatabases()
spark.catalog.listTables("prod")
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    S[Spark Application] -->|SQL Query| C{Catalog / Metastore}
    C -->|Returns File Paths| S
    S -->|Reads actual data| D[(S3 / ADLS Lake)]
    style C fill:#1e3a8a

entries.title: Fine-Grained Access Control (FGAC)
entries.subtitle: Row and Column Level Security
entries.description: Storage-level security (IAM roles) only protects directories (bucket-level). Modern catalogs like Unity Catalog or AWS Lake Formation allow you to write SQL GRANT statements to restrict access to specific rows (e.g., only users in "UK" can see UK rows) or columns (masking SSN).
entries.when_to_use: Required for handling PII (Personally Identifiable Information), GDPR compliance, and multi-tenant data platforms.
entries.syntax: GRANT SELECT ON TABLE customers TO analyst_role;
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# Example of Dynamic View for Row/Column security
# Analysts querying this view only see their own country's data:

spark.sql("""
CREATE VIEW v_sales_secure AS 
SELECT 
  id,
  amount,
  CASE WHEN is_account_group_member('hr') THEN ssn ELSE '***' END AS ssn
FROM sales
WHERE country = current_user_country()
""")

sections.title: Complex Data Types
sections.description: Working with non-scalar data like Arrays, Structs, Maps, and raw JSON strings. Essential for modern API and NoSQL data sources.

entries.title: Explode vs Higher-Order Functions
entries.subtitle: Managing Array columns
entries.description: Arrays can be unnested into multiple rows using explode(), which multiplies the dataset size. Alternatively, Higher-Order Functions (like transform, filter) allow map/reduce operations directly inside the array without adding rows.
entries.when_to_use: Use explode() if you need to group/join by the individual array items. Use transform() if you just need to mutate the array (e.g., multiply all taxes by 1.2) while maintaining the original row count.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import explode, expr

# 1. EXPLODE: 1 row with [A, B] becomes 2 rows
df_flat = df.withColumn("item", explode("items_array"))

# 2. TRANSFORM (Spark 3+): 1 row stays 1 row
df_mapped = df.withColumn(
    "prices_with_tax",
    expr("transform(prices_array, price -> price * 1.21)")
)

entries.title: JSON Parsing: Structs vs Strings
entries.subtitle: from_json vs get_json_object
entries.description: When dealing with a JSON text column, you can either extract a single path as a string (get_json_object) or cast the entire JSON string into a native Spark StructType using a schema (from_json).
entries.when_to_use: Use from_json() with a defined schema whenever possible. It parses the JSON once and stores it in typed columns, which is much faster than calling get_json_object() multiple times on the same row.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import from_json, get_json_object
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StringType, IntegerType

# BAD: Parses JSON string 3 times per row
df.select(
    get_json_object("json_payload", "$.user.age"),
    get_json_object("json_payload", "$.user.name"),
    get_json_object("json_payload", "$.user.city")
)

# GOOD: Parses once into a native Struct
schema = StructType() \
    .add("age", IntegerType()) \
    .add("name", StringType()) \
    .add("city", StringType())

df.withColumn("user_struct", from_json("json_payload", schema)) \
  .select("user_struct.*")
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph get_json_object
        J[Raw JSON String] -->|Parse| E1[Extract A]
        J -->|Parse again| E2[Extract B]
    end
    subgraph from_json
        J2[Raw JSON String] -->|Parse once| S[Native Spark Struct (RAM)]
        S --> EA[Access A instantly]
        S --> EB[Access B instantly]
    end

sections.title: Execution & Shuffle Internals
sections.description: Under the hood of Spark Execution Engine. Understanding Tungsten, memory management, and task speculation.

entries.title: Speculative Execution
entries.subtitle: Dealing with Stragglers
entries.description: If a node in your cluster is struggling (failing disk, network issue), a single task might take 10x longer than the rest (a straggler). Speculative execution notices this and launches a duplicate task on a healthy node. The first one to finish wins.
entries.when_to_use: Turn it on (spark.speculation=true) in unreliable cloud environments or heterogeneous clusters. Be careful if your tasks write to an external database (idempotency is required!).
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# To configure speculation thresholds:
spark.conf.set("spark.speculation", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.speculation.multiplier", "1.5") # 1.5x slower than median
spark.conf.set("spark.speculation.quantile", "0.75")  # Triggers when 75% of tasks are done
entries.diagrama_mermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Normal Tasks
        T1[Task 1: 10s] --> Done1((Done))
        T2[Task 2: 12s] --> Done2((Done))
    end
    subgraph Straggler
        T3[Task 3: 50s...] --> Failed((Killed))
    end
    subgraph Speculative
        S3[Task 3 Copy: 11s] --> Done3((Done))
    end
    T1 ~~~ T3
    T3 -.->|Detected as slow| S3

sections.title: Advanced Optimization Tuning
sections.description: Manual dials to tune Spark when Auto mechanisms (like AQE) are not enough or when statistics are missing.

entries.title: Join Hints
entries.subtitle: Overriding the Catalyst Optimizer
entries.description: Sometimes Spark lacks accurate statistics (e.g. reading from a messy data lake) and fails to realize a table is small enough to broadcast. A Broadcast Hint forces Spark to use a BroadcastHashJoin.
entries.when_to_use: When you identify a slow SortMergeJoin in the UI and you KNOW one side fits easily in executor memory (< 1GB typically, though default threshold is 10MB).
entries.codigo_pyspark:
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Old way:
df_joined = large_df.join(broadcast(small_df), "key")

# New way (Hints):
df_joined = large_df.join(small_df.hint("broadcast"), "key")

# Other hints:
df.hint("merge")        # Forces SortMergeJoin
df.hint("shuffle_hash") # Forces ShuffleHashJoin

entries.title: Files & maxPartitionBytes
entries.subtitle: Tuning Read Partitions
entries.description: When reading hundreds of tiny CSV/JSON files, Spark will map 1 file to 1 partition, causing overhead. You can use spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes (default 128MB) to pack multiple tiny files into a single partition at READ time.
entries.when_to_use: If your read stage has thousands of tasks taking 10ms each, you suffer from tiny-file syndrome. Increase maxPartitionBytes or use file packing.
entries.codigo_pyspark:
# The default is 128MB. 
# If you have 10,000 files of 1MB, Spark packs ~128 files per partition.
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes"))

# If you have a cluster with huge RAM and want fewer, larger partitions:
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes", "256mb")
